limitations: Only deterministic JSON facts were available; no raw hourly series were provided.
Intermediate intraday runs are reconstructed from retained calibration snapshots, so causality for transition effects remains partial.
Three hours were missing from observed calibration coverage, which limits direct verification of late-day handoff behavior.

executiveSummary.headline: Model was close to TEPCO overall, but trailed in overnight and late-afternoon bands
executiveSummary.summary: The model finished with near-parity on daily error: MAE was 319.6 MW vs TEPCO 310.4 MW and WAPE was 1.36% vs 1.33%, with a close verdict. The model won 14 of 24 hours and had better RMSE and much lower max error than TEPCO, but it underperformed in 00-05 and 16-18, while a large 17->18 ramp miss drove the biggest shape error.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Overnight carryover left the model biased low
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: 00-05 had model MAE 220.1 MW versus TEPCO 121.7 MW and model bias of -220.1 MW. Calibration also shows carryoverAdjustmentMw 0.0 and no day-bias correction applied.
counterEvidence: Daily verdict was still close overall, not a severe degradation.
Calibration did not indicate any carryover was applied, so the issue is omission rather than misapplication.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Daytime and peak hours were persistently under-forecast
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: 11-15 had the worst band MAE at 502.8 MW and a -502.8 MW bias, and the top misses at hours 12 and 14 were both large negative errors. The peak hour 19 was also under-forecast by 728.7 MW.
counterEvidence: TEPCO was worse than the model at hour 12, so the bias is not uniform across all comparators.
RMSE remained better than TEPCO, so the miss is localized rather than globally unstable.

rootCauseHypotheses.title: Morning ramp may reflect transition inertia rather than weather
rootCauseHypotheses.explanation: 06-10 had model-better performance with a small -27.0 MW bias, while diagnostics show lag24HourlyDeltaByBand of 666.0 MW and recentSameBusinessTypeDeltaByBand of 867.8 MW. Because the day involved a business-day to non-business-day transition, lag_24h inertia or ramp contamination is a plausible contributor to the ramp shape.
counterEvidence: Morning ramp model bias was only -27.0 MW, so the evidence is not a large direct bias signal.
Weather diagnostics were neutral, so the transition explanation is stronger than a weather-driven one but still indirect.

featureRecommendations.suggestion: Enable carryover when the 00-05 band shows model bias below -150 MW for 3+ consecutive snapshots, using a 0.6 decay into the next day.
featureRecommendations.expectedEffect: Should reduce overnight under-forecasting and improve early-hour handoff stability.
featureRecommendations.risk: May over-carry if the next day starts with a structurally lower load regime.
featureRecommendations.validationPlan: Replay the 16 retained calibration snapshots and compare 00-05 MAE and next-band bias before vs after the carryover trigger.

featureRecommendations.suggestion: Add a daytime scale-up trigger when 11-15 residuals stay below -400 MW and the latest observed-hour adjustment exceeds 300 MW, with a 0.85 shrinkage cap.
featureRecommendations.expectedEffect: Should lift the repeated midday under-forecast and narrow the largest daily misses.
featureRecommendations.risk: Could overshoot on cooler or low-load days if the scale-up is too aggressive.
featureRecommendations.validationPlan: Run a snapshot replay focused on hours 11-15 and 14-19, measuring MAE, bias, and max error against the current calibration path.

operatorNotes: Weather diagnostics were neutral across all bands, so the error pattern is more consistent with regime and intraday shape issues than with temperature shocks.
The model outperformed TEPCO on RMSE and peak-hour timing, but the daily win was still narrow because of overnight and daytime under-forecasting.
Calibration was observed from 21 hours and applied a 415.5 MW base adjustment, but no business-type transition or residual-damping control was applied.
limitations: Only deterministic JSON facts were available; no raw hourly series were provided.
Intermediate intraday runs are reconstructed from retained calibration snapshots, so causality for transition effects remains partial.
Three hours were missing from observed calibration coverage, which limits direct verification of late-day handoff behavior.
